2024 · ACL 2024 · Agents

AppWorld: A Controllable World of Apps and People for Benchmarking Interactive Coding Agents

A fully simulated world of 9 everyday apps and ~100 fictitious people, reachable through 457 APIs, where an agent has to write rich, iterative code against a live environment — and is graded not on its text but on the resulting database state, including collateral damage. The best 2024 system, GPT-4o, finishes under half of the "normal" tasks and just 30% of the "challenge" set.

Trivedi, Khot, Hartmann, Manku, Dong, Li, Gupta, Sabharwal, Balasubramanian
venue ACL 2024 citations 239 topic Agents source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By 2024 a wave of "tool-use" benchmarks could check whether a model picks the right API and fills its arguments. But the tasks they posed were shallow: a simple sequence of API calls, gradeable by matching the call that was made. Real digital assistants don't work that way. Consider the task in the figure below — "play my Spotify playlist with enough songs for the entire workout today." No amount of perfect API knowledge lets you solve this in one shot: the agent has to figure out which note holds today's workout plan, parse a duration out of free-form text, then write a loop that keeps adding songs until their total length covers the session. It has to plan, branch, handle failures, and adapt to what each API returns.

AppWorld calls this a strong interaction requirement: the task cannot, even in principle, be completed by a non-interactive program, because critical inputs only become available by observing the environment. Prior tool benchmarks (ToolLLM, API-Bank, Gorilla and the like) almost never demanded this, and they graded on the API trace rather than on what the actions actually did to the world. Agent environments like WebArena and OSWorld did test interaction, but over web/GUI surfaces — none offered a controllable ecosystem of personal apps populated with people's data and mutable state, where success and side effects can be read directly off a database. That ecosystem, and an evaluation that uses it, is the gap AppWorld fills.

// how it works

The method

AppWorld is two things: an Engine (the simulated world) and a Benchmark (the tasks and grader built on top of it).

AppWorld Engine — a world of apps and people

The Engine is a high-quality execution environment of roughly 60K lines of code: 9 everyday apps — Amazon, Spotify, Venmo, Gmail, Todoist, SimpleNote, Splitwise, a FileSystem, and a Phone — operable through 457 APIs, backed by a single SQLite database of 101 tables / 726 columns / ~370K rows. That database is populated with 106 fictitious people (ages 19–60) wired into realistic relationships — roommates, coworkers, family — with two years of plausible activity (rent split among roommates but not coworkers, and so on). Crucially the world is engineered for control and reproducibility: the whole stack runs serverless in one Python process (FastAPI's TestClient over in-memory SQLite), loads a fresh task DB in under 0.5s, freezes the date and time (freezegun) per task, and sandboxes the agent's Python (gVisor). The 457 APIs are themselves backed by ~1,780 unit tests at 98% coverage, and the docs are auto-generated from the API code so the documentation an agent reads is reliable. Agents act through an IPython-style stateful shell: write a code block, see its output, reuse variables, repeat.

AppWorld Benchmark — 750 tasks from 250 scenarios

The Benchmark (~40K lines of code) is 750 tasks = 250 task scenarios × 3 tasks each. A scenario is a blueprint with placeholders — "I like the last {last-color} {apparel} I bought on Amazon, repurchase the same in that size; prefer {preferred-color} if available" — and a per-scenario Task Generator instantiates concrete tasks by varying both the placeholders and the starting database state. Each generated task is guaranteed to (1) be well-defined (its pre-suppositions hold in the DB), (2) carry distractors (multiple past T-shirt orders, so skipping careful reasoning leads the agent astray), (3) include realistic hurdles (e.g. the default payment card declines), and (4) form a contrast set with its sibling tasks. Tasks span multiple apps (avg 1.8, max 6), draw on many unique APIs (avg 9.5, max 26), and demand rich code — averaging 50 lines, up to 134 — using comprehensions, loops, conditionals, and exception handling.

The signature idea — grade the world, not the words

AppWorld's evaluation is state-based: it ignores the agent's prose and the exact APIs it called, and instead reads what changed in the database. The grader computes a fast, hash-based diff DΔ between the task's start and end states, then checks two conditions against a hand-authored set of expected changes Cexpect (e.g. exactly one order placed, for a T-shirt) and allowed-but-non-mandatory changes Callow (e.g. it's fine if the cart is emptied afterward):

pass(task)  ⇔   Cexpect ⊆ DΔ ⊆ Cexpect ∪ Callow // left inclusion: every required change happened. right inclusion: nothing else did — the collateral-damage check.

The left inclusion says the agent did the job; the right inclusion says it did nothing it shouldn't have — no cancelled wrong order, no message to the wrong person. This is what lets many different valid programs pass the same task while still penalizing side effects, encoded as a battery of programmatic assertions (avg 8, max 22 per task). Two metrics summarize a run: Task Goal Completion (TGC), the percentage of tasks that pass all their tests, and Scenario Goal Completion (SGC), the percentage of scenarios where all three sibling tasks pass — a contrast-set-style consistency bar. The 750 tasks split into Train (105), Dev (60), Test-N (168, "normal") and Test-C (417, "challenge"); Test-C is every task that needs an API from a designated unseen app (Amazon, Gmail), forcing agents to read documentation rather than recall actions from training data or prompts.

Methods and models tested

Six agent methods were evaluated: ReAct (interleave a Thought with a Code step), Plan & Execute, Full Code + Reflexion (write the whole program, then reflect on the stack trace and retry), Iterative Parallel Function Calling, plus the specialized ToolLLaMA and CodeAct. Because the full API documentation is ~142K tokens, an LLM API-predictor first narrows it to the relevant APIs. The first four methods were instantiated with two closed (GPT-4o, GPT-4-Turbo) and two open (LLaMA-3, DeepSeek-Coder) base models; GPT-4 and Gemini 1.5 Pro were dropped after weak, expensive initial runs.

// what they found

The results

The benchmark is hard for everyone. The strongest configuration, ReAct + GPT-4o, reaches just 48.8 TGC on the normal set and 30.2 on the challenge set. Switch to the stricter scenario metric and it falls further — 32.1 and 13.0 SGC — because models do not reliably solve all three variants of the same scenario (a 30–50% drop from TGC to SGC). GPT-4-Turbo trails badly (best 32.7 / 17.5 TGC); the best open model, FullCodeRefl + LLaMA-3, manages 24.4 on Test-N but only 7.0 on Test-C; DeepSeek-Coder is weaker still. The two narrowly fine-tuned systems, CodeAct (Mistral-7B) and ToolLLaMA, score 0.0 on every split — specialized tool-training simply doesn't transfer to this open-ended setting.

Base LLM · MethodTGC NSGC NTGC CSGC C
GPT-4o · ReAct48.832.130.213.0
GPT-4o · PlanExec44.623.219.77.9
GPT-4o · FullCodeRefl33.926.819.212.2
GPT-4-Turbo · PlanExec32.716.111.03.6
LLaMA-3 · FullCodeRefl24.417.97.04.3
DeepSeek-Coder · FullCodeRefl13.18.95.82.9
Mistral-7B · CodeAct0.00.00.00.0
LLaMA · ToolLLaMA0.00.00.00.0

// Table 3 (selected rows). N = Test-N (normal), C = Test-C (challenge). TGC = task goal completion, SGC = scenario goal completion.

The wall is composing code, not finding APIs

Two analyses locate the difficulty. First, performance degrades monotonically with task hardness: ReAct's TGC slides from 58.3 at difficulty level 1 to 21.0 at level 3, and tasks that need 60+ lines of solution code score under 20 TGC, with the same downward trend across number of unique APIs and number of apps. Second — and this is the key diagnosis — API retrieval is not the bottleneck. Replacing the predicted APIs with oracle APIs (the exact ones used in the gold solution) lifts TGC by at most 7.8 points on Test-N and 9.8 on Test-C, topping out at 54.8 / 35.2 — and the API predictor was already strong (87 / 71 F1). So the agents mostly know which APIs to use; what defeats them is using those APIs inside complex, interactive code and adapting to the errors and messages the environment returns.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

AppWorld's evaluation design is unusually principled — execution over a controllable database, with an explicit collateral-damage clause and a contrast-set consistency metric — and it's backed by serious engineering (100K+ lines over 14 months, 1,780 API unit tests at 98% coverage, gold solutions validated end-to-end). The caveats are mostly about how far the numbers generalize, not about whether the harness is sound.

The most important one is age. The headline "GPT-4o solves ~49%" is a mid-2024 snapshot taken before reasoning models existed. The baselines (GPT-4o, GPT-4-Turbo, LLaMA-3, DeepSeek-Coder) and scaffolds (ReAct, Reflexion, Plan & Execute) were reasonable then, but o1/o3-class reasoning, Claude 3.5+/3.7, GPT-5 and modern coding agents were never run — the live leaderboard at appworld.dev, not this table, is where to read current difficulty. And the specialized systems scoring a literal 0.0 say more about narrow fine-tuning than about a calibrated floor.

The deeper structural caveat is that this is a fully simulated world with clean, auto-documented APIs. Real APIs are messier — missing or stale response schemas, auth quirks, rate limits — and the paper only ablates the harder "no response schema" setting in an appendix rather than treating it as a first-class condition. The grader, too, is exactly as complete as its hand-authored Cexpect/Callow sets across 250 scenarios: an unanticipated-but-valid solution path could be flagged as collateral damage (a false negative), and an un-listed side effect could slip through (a false positive). The validation solutions and 98% coverage mitigate this, but it rests on author foresight rather than measurement.

baselines

Fair for mid-2024 (GPT-4o, GPT-4-Turbo, LLaMA-3, DeepSeek-Coder; ReAct/Reflexion/PlanExec scaffolds), but pre-reasoning-era — no o1/o3, Claude 3.5+, or GPT-5. ToolLLaMA/CodeAct at 0.0 are uncalibrated floors. Read appworld.dev for current numbers.

contamination

Handled well for a static benchmark: Test-C demands unseen-app APIs so memorization fails, grading is state-based not answer-matching, and tasks are procedurally instantiated. But the Engine + gold solutions are public on GitHub, so future training-set leakage is a real risk, and the unseen-app guard covers only two apps.

what's missed

A clean simulated world isn't a messy real one (the realistic "no response schema" case is only an appendix ablation); API-only means no UI/browser/mobile grounding; single-supervisor only (no multi-agent coordination); and grader correctness is bounded by hand-written expected/allowed change sets per scenario.

would raise confidence

Current frontier + reasoning-model scores; a never-released held-out test set (gold solutions and generators kept private); a human audit of grader false-positives/negatives on "passed" vs "failed" trajectories; and the response-schema-ablated setting run as a headline condition, not an appendix.

// bottom line

The specific scores are dated, but AppWorld's contribution was never the leaderboard — it was the evaluation template. Moving tool-use assessment from "did you call the right API" to "did the world end up in the right state, with no collateral damage," graded by execution over a controllable database, is the methodological idea that the later interactive-agent and tool-use benchmarks (MCP-Universe, Terminal-Bench, and the broader MCP/SWE-benchmark lineage) inherited.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Tool use, reframed as interactive coding. AppWorld is a simulated world of 9 apps, 457 APIs and ~100 interrelated people, with 750 tasks where an agent writes rich, iterative code against a live, stateful environment — not a single sequence of API calls.
  2. The evaluation is the idea: grade the database, not the text. A task passes only if every required change happened and nothing else did (the collateral-damage clause), so many valid programs pass while side effects are caught; SGC adds an all-three-variants consistency bar.
  3. It's hard for the right reason. GPT-4o tops out at 48.8 / 30.2 TGC (normal / challenge), oracle APIs help by ≤9.8 points, and scores fall smoothly with code length and app count — the wall is composing APIs into correct, error-adaptive code, not retrieving them.
